text:
For 20 years, SaaS was a fixed-margin business. You sold a seat, the marginal cost of serving that seat was rounding-error close to zero, and finance reviewed pricing once a year because pricing didn't really *do* anything between reviews.

AI broke that. Quietly.

Every AI feature you ship has a token cost, a compute cost, an inference cost. Variable COGS are back in software for the first time since on-prem. And most teams haven't internalized what that means yet.

Most people are reading the shift as "we need usage-based pricing." That's half the answer and it's the easy half. The harder shift is that pricing is no longer a finance artifact reviewed annually. It's a product surface — something you reprice and repackage multiple times a year as your model costs, model choices, and customer behavior change underneath you.

My 12-24 month prediction: pure-subscription AI products will quietly bleed margin and lose to hybrid peers (subscription floor + usage on the AI-heavy surfaces). Outcome-based pricing will get loud in 2026, but the version that actually works will be a base subscription plus performance upside — paired with services. Pure "pay for results" will keep failing because nobody can agree on attribution.

What to do Monday: get your pricing on the product roadmap. Literally. Put it in the quarterly planning doc. Assign a PM to it.

If your CFO still owns pricing alone, you're already behind.

Where are you drawing the line between PM-owned and finance-owned pricing decisions right now?

#SaaSPricing #ProductStrategy #PLG
